sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Hyperglycaemia is elevated blood glucose and may occur in diabetes due to insulin deficiency, intercurrent illness, missed therapy, or impaired insulin action. Severe hyperglycaemia may accompany diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) or hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS).

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, blurred vision
- Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain
- Confusion, drowsiness, weakness
- Kussmaul respirations or acetone breath in DKA
- Dehydration, hypotension, shock, or coma in severe cases

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- HHS is more common in older patients with type 2 diabetes and carries high mortality
- DKA classically occurs in type 1 diabetes but can also occur in type 2 diabetes, including euglycaemic DKA with SGLT2 inhibitors
- Newly diagnosed diabetes, inability to tolerate fluids, moderate to severe dehydration, pregnancy, or suspected intercurrent illness all require hospital review
- Abdominal pain, vomiting, altered mental state, marked dehydration, or Kussmaul breathing should increase concern for DKA or HHS

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position the patient to maintain the airway if indicated
- Give oxygen if clinically indicated
- Perform repeated vital signs with regular monitoring of BGL and clinical state
- Monitor BGL, GCS, SpO2, and ketones where available
- Assess hydration where appropriate
- Maintain a low threshold for consulting the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain a 12 lead ECG
- Establish vascular access
- Consider cautious IV fluid replacement where dehydration is present, with ongoing reassessment and senior review
- Insulin therapy for DKA or HHS should be hospital-led or specifically doctor-directed; do not make routine field insulin administration part of standard prehospital hyperglycaemia care

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- DKA is more common in type 1 diabetes; HHS is more common in type 2 diabetes and older patients
- Both are medical emergencies with high morbidity and mortality if untreated
